does: This paper proposes a method to improve the generalization of reward models used in RLHF by regularizing hidden states during reward model training. The core contribution is retaining the base language model's text-generation head alongside a newly trained reward head, using text-generation losses to preserve the hidden states' original capabilities while learning reward predictions. The approach prevents the hidden states from shifting away from their text-generation-optimized representations during reward model fine-tuning. Experimental results show this regularization technique substantially improves reward model accuracy across various evaluation settings and reduces reward over-optimization, where excessively optimizing rewards leads to degraded actual model performance. The method is straightforward to implement as an architectural and training modification that constrains how much reward training can alter the base model's learned representations.
connects: This work builds on the established framework of RLHF for aligning LLMs with human preferences, extending beyond prior approaches that primarily addressed reward over-optimization through policy-side constraints like KL divergence penalties. The paper responds to recognized limitations in reward model generalization across distribution shifts, a problem that has received less direct attention than policy optimization constraints. It connects to broader research on representation learning and multi-task learning, where maintaining task-relevant information across auxiliary objectives is valuable. The approach also relates to work on continual learning and catastrophic forgetting, applying similar principles of regularization to prevent task-specific fine-tuning from corrupting learned representations. Previous studies have shown that reward models often fail on out-of-distribution examples, motivating the paper's focus on improving generalization through architectural design rather than just policy modification.
enables: This work enables more robust reward models that better generalize to unseen prompts and responses, reducing the reward over-optimization problem and improving the practical effectiveness of RLHF. The regularization approach could facilitate stronger alignment by making reward signals more reliable across diverse model outputs. Future research could extend this to investigate what other auxiliary objectives or constraints preserve useful hidden state properties, explore theoretical understanding of how hidden state regularization affects the reward landscape, or apply similar multi-head training strategies to other alignment techniques beyond RLHF. The method could enable more efficient exploration of the policy optimization landscape by providing more stable reward signals. It may also inspire investigation into other properties of hidden states worth preserving during specialized fine-tuning, potentially leading to more general frameworks for training specialized model heads without degrading foundational capabilities.
